name: Czechia
summary: Very safe (GPI 2025: 11th), cheap to live in, with a strong passport, low 15% flat income tax, dual citizenship retained both ways (allowed since 2014; Czechia on Bangladesh's DNC list) and a growing Prague IT hub — but a poor fit for a Muslim Bangladeshi couple: the Muslim community is negligible (~0.2%, no purpose-built mosque, real Islamophobia), politics turned hostile (ANO/Babiš won the 2025 election and is courting the far-right SPD), citizenship takes ~10 years with B1 Czech, non-EU students are locked out of public health insurance, and Czech gates daily life. Derived overall sits below the 60% inclusion line — flagged as a drop candidate.

links.title: Study in Czechia — official national portal (DZS/MŠMT)
Ministry of Interior — Citizenship of the Czech Republic (official)

job-market.summary: Prague is a growing IT hub (Red Hat, Microsoft, Honeywell, Avast) amid a 200,000+ labour shortage with 63% of IT firms struggling to hire, but salaries are low in absolute terms (offset by low cost) and Czech often helps outside MNCs.

pros.text: Strong tech demand: ICT employment is set to grow ~9.6% to 2030 (above the EU average), 63% of IT companies struggle to find skilled staff, and ~15,000 new tech jobs open yearly within a 200,000+ overall labour shortage.
Prague hosts major employers (Red Hat, Microsoft, Honeywell, Avast); a senior SDE at Microsoft Prague earns a median ~CZK 2.29m.

cons.text: Software-engineer pay is low in absolute terms (~€34k–48k in Prague; AI roles ~€40k–78k), though partly offset by low living costs.
The market is Prague-centric (Brno secondary), and Czech is commonly expected outside multinationals, limiting reach for a new arrival.

links.title: Nucamp — Getting a job in tech in Czech Republic (2025)
Levels.fyi — Software Engineer salary, Prague

visa-access.summary: Bangladeshi students lodge at VFS Dhaka but the long-term visa is decided by the Czech Embassy in New Delhi via a capacity-constrained email-registration appointment system; funds proof is modest and the permit needs annual renewal.

pros.text: Applications are lodged at VFS Global in Dhaka (Gulshan), and the proof-of-funds bar for students is modest; processing is typically 15–30 days.
No Bangladesh-specific visa ban; the EU degree and central location make it a low-cost base.

cons.text: Czechia has no decision-making mission in Bangladesh — the long-term student visa is decided by the Embassy in New Delhi, and appointment slots are released via a constrained email-registration system on set dates.
The long-term student visa is valid only ~12 months and must be renewed annually for the whole degree.

links.title: Embassy of the Czech Republic, New Delhi — long-term visa appointments for Bangladesh (official)
Czech Universities — How to apply for a Czech student visa

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship is retained both ways (allowed since 2014) and the passport is top-tier (Henley 7th), but naturalisation takes ~10 years and requires B1 Czech plus a Czech Life & Institutions exam.

pros.text: Dual citizenship works both ways: Czechia has allowed multiple nationality since 1 January 2014 and Czechia is on Bangladesh's DNC list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
The Czech passport ranks 7th on Henley 2026 (~182–183 destinations), and the dual-citizenship framework has been stable since 2014.

cons.text: Naturalisation requires ~10 years of residence (including ~5 years of permanent residence).
Applicants must pass a B1 Czech-language exam plus a Czech Life & Institutions test — a real burden for English-first applicants.

links.title: Ministry of Interior — Citizenship of the Czech Republic (official)
GLOBALCIT — Czech dual-citizenship reform

post-study-work.summary: Graduates get a 9-month job-seeker residence permit, then an Employee Card or EU Blue Card on a qualifying offer — a short but workable runway, available to each graduating spouse.

pros.text: Non-EU graduates can stay up to 9 months on a job-seeker residence permit to find work or start a business, with both graduating spouses eligible.
Conversion is offer-based and deterministic via the Employee Card (combined work-and-residence permit) or the EU Blue Card for higher-paid roles.

cons.text: The 9-month job-search window is short next to Canada's 3-year PGWP or Germany's 18 months.
The Employee Card ties the holder to a specific qualified position initially.

links.title: Study in Czechia — Work while or after studying (official portal)
Study-Abroad — Graduate careers in Czech Republic 2026

spouse-family.summary: Family reunification and free schooling exist, and the spouse of an Employee Card holder can work, but the accompanying spouse of a student faces conditions; if both study, each holds an independent track.

pros.text: The spouse of an Employee Card holder can obtain labour-market access, and if both spouses study each holds their own permit and job-seeker route.
Children attend public school free of charge; family reunification is available subject to income and housing.

cons.text: Family reunification for a student principal carries income/housing conditions, and the dependent spouse's work access is not automatic during the study phase.

links.title: Study in Czechia — Living & family (official portal)

msc-study.summary: Solid English-taught CS/AI options at CTU Prague and Charles University at moderate tuition, with low living costs and a one-year master qualifying for the job-seeker permit.

pros.text: CTU Prague and Charles University offer respected English-taught CS/AI master's (≈7 AI master's nationally); English-programme tuition is moderate (broadly ~€2k–15k/yr) and Czech-taught study is tuition-free.
A Bangladeshi BSc-CSE is admissible at the graduate level, living costs are low, and a master's qualifies for the job-seeker permit.

cons.text: The English-taught catalogue is narrower than Canada/UK, and well-funded scholarships for non-EU master's students are limited.

links.title: Study.eu — Master's in the Czech Republic (2026)
Charles University — Tuition fees (official)

pr-pathway.summary: Five years of continuous residence leads to permanent residence, a clear Employee-Card-based route eased by labour shortages, with an A2-Czech requirement and a restrictive political backdrop.

pros.text: Permanent residence is reachable after 5 years of continuous residence, with study and work time building the route via the Employee Card.
Acute labour shortages keep skilled-worker channels open and the income bars attainable for engineers.

cons.text: Permanent residence requires an A2 Czech-language exam, and the ANO-led government signals a more restrictive migration direction.

links.title: Study in Czechia — Long-term residence / staying after studies (official)

income-cost.summary: Low living costs and a 15% flat income tax leave a workable surplus despite modest salaries, with housing cheaper than Western Europe though rising in Prague.

pros.text: A 15% flat income tax (to ~CZK 1.76m) plus low living costs let two modest tech salaries net a workable surplus; relocation and study-phase costs are low.

cons.text: Absolute salaries are low, so savings/remittance potential is moderate rather than strong, and Prague rents have been rising.

links.title: Accace — 2026 Tax Guideline for the Czech Republic

healthcare.summary: Good-quality care once employed (mandatory public insurance), but non-EU students are excluded from the public system and must buy gappy commercial insurance — a real newcomer barrier.

pros.text: Employment triggers mandatory, good-quality public health insurance (VZP); from 1 January 2026 the state even covers contributions for unemployed Employee-Card holders, closing a long-standing gap.

cons.text: Non-EU students cannot join public insurance and must buy commercial cover (PVZP/Maxima, ~€280–520/yr) that is known for exclusions and waiting periods — a genuine study-phase barrier.

links.title: Study in Czechia — Medical care and insurance (official)
VisaHQ — Czech state to pay health insurance for unemployed Employee-Card holders (2026)

culture-language.summary: Prague's IT sector is English-friendly and the city is highly livable, but Czech is essential for daily life and status, and the society is ethnically homogeneous with little exposure to South-Asian Muslims.

pros.text: Prague is highly livable and its IT workplaces are English-friendly, with good work-life balance and a central European location.

cons.text: Czech is required for daily life and for status (A2 for PR, B1 for citizenship), with a steep learning curve for English-first arrivals.
The society is ethnically homogeneous with little exposure to South-Asian Muslim newcomers, so social acceptance can be cool.

links.title: Study in Czechia — Living in Czechia (official)

safety-law.summary: One of the world's safest countries (GPI 2025: 11th, 1.435) with upper-tier rule of law (WJP 2025: 20th), but with notable Islamophobic incidents and weak protection of Muslim minorities.

pros.text: Global Peace Index 2025: 11th of 163 (score 1.435) — among the world's safest — with low violent crime and stable institutions; WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 20th of 143.
Strong personal and women's safety with reliable civil justice.

cons.text: Islamophobia is a documented problem (a Brno mosque defaced with death threats; ~31% of Muslim job-seekers report discrimination), and anti-discrimination protection for Muslims is weakly enforced.

links.title: Vision of Humanity — Global Peace Index 2025
World Justice Project — Czech Republic country profile (2025)

politics.summary: Institutions are stable, but politics turned hostile to migration: Andrej Babiš's ANO won the October 2025 election and is courting the far-right SPD, with a consistent anti-EU-migration line.

pros.text: Institutions are stable and democratic, and labour-shortage-driven skilled-worker channels (Employee Card, Blue Card) remain operational.

cons.text: ANO (Babiš) won the October 2025 election and is seeking a government with the far-right SPD; both reject EU migration pacts and quotas, hardening the climate for non-EU newcomers.
Anti-Islam rhetoric features prominently in extremist and disinformation discourse, targeting Muslim migrants specifically.

links.title: Expats.cz — Czech election 2025: where parties stand

tax.summary: A low 15% flat income tax (23% above ~€70k) and moderate social wedge are attractive, though there is no expat regime and no confirmed Czech–Bangladesh tax treaty.

pros.text: Income tax is a low 15% flat rate up to ~CZK 1.76m (23% above), assessed individually with no second-earner penalty — light by EU standards.

cons.text: There is no special expat/inbound-worker tax relief, and no Czech–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty is confirmed (Bangladesh has DTAs with only ~36 countries), so repatriation relief is uncertain.

links.title: PwC — Czech Republic: individual taxes

muslim-diaspora.summary: The weakest dimension: Czechia's Muslim community is negligible (~0.2%, ~20,000 people), there is no purpose-built mosque, halal options are scarce, and Islamophobia is a documented problem with essentially no Bangladeshi community.

pros.text: Prague has a couple of prayer rooms and a handful of halal restaurants in the centre, sufficient for basic practice.

cons.text: Muslims are only ~0.2% of the population (~20,000, mostly Turkish/Arab) with no purpose-built mosque and scarce halal infrastructure, and essentially no established Bangladeshi community.
Islamophobia is documented (mosque death-threat graffiti in Brno; ~31% of Muslim job-seekers report discrimination) amid a 'phantom Muslim menace' disinformation climate.

links.title: Islam in the Czech Republic (overview)
Radio Prague International — Hidden Muslim communities in Prague

direct-work-route.summary: A workable direct-work route for a BD software engineer, with two paths of differing friction. The EU Blue Card is the clean primary route — no quota, the applicant books a New Delhi appointment and applies directly, needs a 3-year degree (both hold BSc-CSE) and salary ≥1.5× average (CZK 73,823/mo / ~€2,960 from 1 May 2026), which a Prague/Brno tech salary clears. The Employee Card via the Highly Qualified Worker Programme (open to all third countries, CZ-ISCO groups 1–3) is the employer-led alternative, but since Oct 2025 the Delhi embassy accepts it only through a government programme, the Delhi Employee-Card quota (1,560/yr) is shared across the whole subcontinent, BD documents need superlegalization, and the highly-skilled fast-track pilot excludes Bangladesh. The route links cleanly onward — Blue/Employee-Card time counts toward PR at 5 years (A2 Czech) and citizenship at ~10 years (B1), dual retained both ways. Direction is net-expanding for skilled labour despite the ANO win, but the BD track record is thin and the political climate hostile.

pros.text: EU Blue Card is a clean direct-hire route from Dhaka: no quota, the applicant applies in person at New Delhi (no government-programme gate), needs only a 3-year university degree — both hold BSc-CSE — and a salary ≥1.5× average (CZK 73,823/mo / ~€2,960 from 1 May 2026) that a Prague/Brno tech wage clears; 90-day decision.

Clean onward linkage: Blue/Employee-Card residence counts toward permanent residence at 5 years (A2 Czech) and citizenship at ~10 years (B1 Czech), with dual citizenship retained both ways (Czechia allows it since 2014; Czechia on Bangladesh's DNC list).

Direction is net-expanding for skilled labour: the 1 June 2026 MPO reform loosened paperwork and widened access to the Highly Qualified Employee and Key & Scientific Personnel programmes, +2,000 Qualified-Employee slots for 2026, and an EU Single-Permit 90-day processing cap — policy explicitly favours skilled over unskilled migration.

cons.text: The Employee Card route is friction-heavy for a BD national: since Oct 2025 the Delhi embassy accepts it only through a government economic-migration programme (no ad-hoc/courier filing), the Delhi Employee-Card quota (1,560/yr) is shared across India/Bangladesh/Nepal/Sri Lanka/Maldives/Bhutan, and BD public documents need superlegalization (heavier than India's apostille).

Bangladesh is excluded from the accelerated highly-skilled / digital-nomad fast-track pilot (Australia/Canada/Japan/UK/US etc. only), and the July-2025 Delhi allocation gave just 24 visa slots for IT/science professionals — a tight subcontinent-wide cap.

The BD direct skilled-work track record to Czechia is thin and emerging rather than established (modest, partly transit/irregular flow; 'only half stay'), so the route is less proven for this nationality than the Indian/non-EU IT inflow that proxies it; the ANO/SPD government adds a hostile backdrop.
